title: The Founding
bullets: In May 1388, General Yi Seong-gye turned his army around at Wihwa Island in the Yalu River instead of invading Ming China — and four years later overthrew Goryeo to found the Joseon dynasty that would rule Korea for the next 518 years.

Joseon adopted Neo-Confucianism as its official state ideology in 1394, replacing Buddhism as the religion of state and triggering a comprehensive Buddhist suppression that confiscated temple lands, dissolved hundreds of monasteries, and exiled monks from the new capital.

Jeong Do-jeon was the chief Neo-Confucian ideologue of the founding — designing the new capital, drafting the legal code, and arguing that the king should be a moral figurehead while scholar-officials governed — until Yi Bang-won murdered him and two royal half-brothers in the First Strife of Princes in 1398.

The new capital at Hanyang (modern Seoul) was sited inside a basin ringed by Mount Bugak, Mount Inwang, Mount Namsan, and Mount Naksan, with Gyeongbokgung Palace, the Jongmyo royal shrine, and the Sungkyunkwan Confucian academy laid out along the geomantic axis.

Yi Bang-won took the throne as Taejong in 1400, consolidated royal authority, and in 1418 voluntarily abdicated to his son Sejong — a remarkable act that produced the smoothest royal succession in Korean history and set up the dynasty's golden age.

Joseon's first century saw the construction of Gyeongbokgung Palace, Changdeokgung Palace, the Jongmyo royal ancestral shrine, the Sungkyunkwan national academy, and an 11-mile city wall whose four major gates were named for the cardinal Confucian virtues.
subtitle: Yi Seong-gye's coup, the move from Buddhism to Confucianism, and Hanyang as the new capital

title: Sejong the Great
bullets: King Sejong, the third son who inherited the throne in 1418 because his older brothers were either incompetent or disinterested, presided over the most concentrated period of state-sponsored cultural and scientific achievement in any premodern East Asian reign.

The Hall of Worthies — a state-funded research institute housed in Gyeongbokgung Palace — produced agricultural manuals, medical encyclopedias, and the technical scholarship behind Hangul, with Sejong personally participating in late-night working sessions.

In 1443 Sejong promulgated Hangul, a 28-letter phonetic alphabet whose consonants are stylized diagrams of the mouth shapes that produce each sound — an alphabet that the linguist Jared Diamond called 'the world's best alphabet.'

Sejong's preface to the Hunminjeongeum justified the new alphabet on explicitly democratic grounds, declaring that 'a wise man can acquaint himself with these letters before the morning is over; even a stupid man can learn them in the space of ten days.'

Jang Yeong-sil, born a government slave for his metallurgical talent, was freed by Sejong and produced the world's first standardized rain gauge (cheugugi, 1441), the self-striking water clock Jagyeongnu, large bronze armillary spheres, and improved metal printing type.

Sejong's generals Kim Jong-seo and Choe Yun-deok drove Jurchen tribes north of the Yalu and Tumen rivers and established the Six Forts and Four Districts that fixed the Korea-China border at roughly its modern shape — a fifteenth-century military campaign whose results have lasted nearly six hundred years.

The yangban scholarly establishment hated Hangul, treated it as a low-prestige 'vernacular script' suitable for women and Buddhist monks, and continued writing all serious literature in classical Chinese for the next 450 years until Korean nationalism in the late nineteenth century finally rescued the alphabet.

While Ming China was actively reducing scientific work after the great voyages of Zheng He, fifteenth-century Korea under Sejong was the leading scientific producer in East Asia — a brief flowering that did not survive his death.
subtitle: Hangul, Jang Yeong-sil's inventions, and the brief moment when Korea led East Asia in science

title: The Yangban Order
bullets: The yangban — Joseon's hereditary scholar-gentry class — were exempted from taxes and military service, expected to dedicate their lives to Confucian study, and dominated the gwageo civil service exam through which they continuously reproduced themselves as the ruling class.

The gwageo exam selected officials based on mastery of the Confucian classics in three rounds, with a typical year producing only thirty-three palace exam passers out of tens of thousands of national candidates — meritocratic in form, hereditary in practice.

Joseon's Confucianization sharply reduced the legal status of women, replacing the relatively flexible bilateral kinship system of Goryeo with strict patrilineal succession, banned widow remarriage for yangban families, and the seclusion of women in inner quarters.

Roughly a third of the early-Joseon population were nobi — hereditary slaves divided into government nobi (gwannobi) who served palace and office and private nobi (sanobi) owned by yangban households — making Joseon one of the most heavily enslaved societies of the early-modern world.

Four major Literati Purges between 1498 and 1545 killed hundreds of Sarim Confucian scholars, including a uniquely Korean episode in which the corpse of the dead Sarim master Kim Jong-jik was beheaded as judicial punishment by King Yeonsangun.

Yi Hwang (Toegye, 1501-1570) and Yi I (Yulgok, 1536-1584) were the great mid-Joseon Neo-Confucian philosophers — both now on Korean banknotes — whose Four-Seven Debate over the metaphysics of moral feeling shaped East Asian thought for three centuries.

Yulgok's ten-thousand-soldier proposal urging Joseon to maintain a permanent standing army against the Japanese threat was rejected by the court as alarmist; twelve years after his death, the Imjin War vindicated him appallingly.

The seowon network of private Confucian academies — over 600 by the late dynasty — provided the institutional power base for the Sarim philosophical lineages and accumulated the same kind of tax-exempt landed wealth that the displaced Buddhist temples had once held.
subtitle: Civil service exams, four classes, the women's collapse, and the philosophical schools that became political factions

title: The Imjin War
bullets: In April 1592, Toyotomi Hideyoshi landed roughly 158,000 Japanese troops armed with European-style matchlock muskets at Busan, took Hanyang in two months, and stated his intention to conquer not just Korea but China and possibly India.

Admiral Yi Sun-sin had spent the year before the invasion building the geobukseon turtle ships — heavily armored warships with iron-spiked roofs to prevent boarding, dragon-head prows, and broadsides bristling with cannons — and won every major naval engagement he fought.

At Hansan Island in August 1592, Yi Sun-sin used a feigned retreat and a crane-wing formation to destroy 47 of 73 Japanese warships and roughly 9,000 Japanese sailors and marines, severing Hideyoshi's seaborne supply line and turning the war.

After Yi was framed and demoted in 1597 and his replacement lost 122 of 134 Joseon ships at Chilcheollyang, he was reinstated with the surviving thirteen ships — and at the Battle of Myeongnyang in October 1597, used the strongest tidal current in Korean waters to sink 31 of 133 Japanese ships without losing a single Korean vessel.

Yi Sun-sin was killed by a Japanese musket bullet at the Battle of Noryang in December 1598, dying on deck after ordering his nephew not to announce the death until the battle was won — a battle that destroyed roughly half the retreating Japanese fleet.

Civilian guerrilla forces called Uibyeong (Righteous Armies) — yangban scholars, Buddhist monks, and farmers — harassed Japanese supply lines from mountain fortresses across Korea, with General Gwon Yul defending Haengju Fortress in 1593 against 30,000 Japanese troops with help from the hwacha rocket-launcher.

The Ming dynasty sent over 40,000 troops under Li Rusong who recaptured Pyongyang in early 1593 and stalemated the Japanese army; the war cost the Ming 8 to 25 million ounces of silver and helped trigger the dynasty's collapse fifty years later.

Retreating Japanese armies systematically captured 50,000 to 100,000 Korean potters, papermakers, weavers, and printers — the Korean potters founded the Arita and Satsuma porcelain traditions that became Japan's first major export to Europe in the seventeenth century.
subtitle: Hideyoshi invades, Yi Sun-sin's turtle ships, the crane-wing formation, and the war that defined Korean memory for four centuries

title: The Manchu Trauma
bullets: In 1627 the Later Jin invaded Korea with 30,000 cavalry, forced King Injo to flee to Ganghwa Island, and extracted recognition as a 'brother state' — but the Joseon court continued to favor the dying Ming and refused to fully break with its old benefactor.

In December 1636, Hong Taiji invaded in person with 120,000 troops — now styled Qing emperor — and trapped King Injo for 47 days of subzero winter siege at the mountain fortress of Namhansanseong, 15 miles southeast of Hanyang.

On January 30, 1637, at Samjeondo on the south bank of the Han River, King Injo descended in commoner's white robes and performed the sambae gugodu — three kneelings and nine prostrations against the cold ground — before Hong Taiji, accepting Qing vassalage in one of the most psychologically traumatic ceremonies in Korean political memory.

The Joseon yangban responded ideologically with the sojunghwa doctrine — that Joseon was now the only remaining authentic Confucian civilization on earth, since the actual Chinese capital was occupied by barbarian Manchu emperors — and continued using Ming era-names in court documents long after the Ming had ceased to exist.

Crown Prince Sohyeon spent eight years as a Manchu hostage in Mukden and Beijing, befriended the Jesuit Adam Schall von Bell, learned Western astronomy, returned to Korea in 1645 with Catholic books and Western scientific instruments — and died suddenly two months later, almost certainly poisoned by his father King Injo, who feared his foreign sympathies.

After the Manchu trauma, Joseon adopted the strict Sea Ban Policy that earned the country its 'Hermit Kingdom' nickname — banning private overseas trade, restricting Japanese merchants to Busan's Waegwan, and producing the most isolated state in early-modern East Asia.

Dutch sailor Hendrick Hamel and 35 crewmates from the Sperwer were shipwrecked on Jeju Island in 1653, held in Korea for thirteen years, escaped to Japan in 1666, and published the Journal of Hendrick Hamel — the West's best Korea source for the next two hundred years.
subtitle: Two Manchu invasions, the kowtow at Samjeondo, the murdered crown prince, and the Hermit Kingdom takes shape

title: Silhak and Late-Joseon Culture
bullets: The Silhak (Practical Learning) movement emerged among scholars who argued that mainstream Neo-Confucian metaphysical disputes were intellectual luxuries Korea could no longer afford and that scholarship should produce usable knowledge in agriculture, governance, and technology.

Yu Hyeong-won, the founding figure of Silhak, spent twenty years in rural retirement writing the Bangye Surok — a sweeping reform proposal advocating land redistribution, the abolition of slavery, and a meritocratic civil service open to all classes.

King Jeongjo founded the Gyujanggak royal library inside Changdeokgung in 1776, modeled on Sejong's Hall of Worthies, and used it as a counterweight to the entrenched yangban political establishment by recruiting talented young officials of mixed factional background.

Jeong Yak-yong (Dasan), the greatest Silhak polymath, designed the pulley crane that built Hwaseong Fortress, wrote on smallpox vaccination, produced 500 books on governance — and spent eighteen years in exile after the Catholic persecutions of 1801.

Hwaseong Fortress at Suwon was built in just 33 months under Jeongjo using Dasan's pulley cranes, paid wage labor instead of corvée, and a fully documented construction record book — and is now a UNESCO World Heritage site.

Jeong Seon pioneered jingyeong sansu (true-view landscape) painting — depicting actual Korean mountains like Mount Inwang and Mount Geumgang rather than imagined Chinese scenes — with sharp vertical brushstrokes that captured the angular granite peaks of the Korean landscape.

Kim Hong-do (Danwon) painted the great genre scenes of late Joseon — wrestling matches, blacksmiths, schoolyards, tile-roof builders — leaving an unmatched visual record of eighteenth-century Korean commoner life.

Catholicism reached Korea without missionary involvement — Yi Seung-hun was baptized in Beijing in 1784 and returned to organize an underground Korean Catholic church that grew to several thousand members within a decade — the only such case in the history of Christian expansion.

The Sinyu Persecution of 1801, the persecutions of 1839, and the great Byeong-in Persecution of 1866 killed thousands of Korean Catholics including the first Korean priest Andrew Kim Taegon and nine French missionaries — but failed to eradicate the church, which underground became modern Korea's 5 million Catholics.
subtitle: Practical learning, Hwaseong Fortress, true-view painting, and the Catholic church that organized itself without priests

title: Faction Wars and the Slow Decline
bullets: Joseon court politics from 1575 onward were dominated by sustained factional struggle — Easterners and Westerners traced philosophical lineages back to Toegye and Yulgok respectively, then split further into Northerners and Southerners, then again into Old Doctrine (Noron) and Young Doctrine (Soron).

The two Yesong Ritual Controversies of 1659 and 1674 split the court into rival camps over how long the dowager queen should mourn her stepson and grandson — questions of pure Confucian ritual that resulted in executions, exiles, and the rise and fall of two government majorities.

King Yeongjo reigned for 52 years (1724-1776) — the longest reign in Joseon history — and tried to break the factional pattern with his Tangpyeong (Impartiality) policy of cross-factional cabinets that gave Joseon its longest period of court stability since the founding generation.

In summer 1762, Yeongjo sealed his mentally ill son Crown Prince Sado in a wooden rice chest in the courtyard of Changgyeonggung Palace for eight days until Sado died of dehydration and heat — one of the most psychologically disturbing royal episodes in any premodern monarchy.

Lady Hyegyeong, Crown Prince Sado's wife, wrote the Hanjungnok ('Memoirs Written in Silence'), an unsparing account of her husband's mental illness and her father-in-law's cruelty that survives as one of the most important first-person accounts of any royal court in East Asian history.

After King Jeongjo's sudden death in 1800 (possibly poisoned), the Sedo Politics era began — the in-law clans of three child kings ran the government as a private patronage system for sixty years, selling office positions for cash and gutting the rural economy through tax extortion.

The Hong Gyeong-rae Rebellion of 1811-1812 was the largest internal revolt Joseon had faced since the Imjin War — a yangban-led northwestern uprising against Sedo corruption and regional discrimination that took the central government four months and a tunneled gunpowder mine to suppress.
subtitle: Old Doctrine vs. Young Doctrine, the rice chest, and the in-law clans that hollowed out the dynasty

title: Opening, Annexation, End
bullets: Choe Je-u founded the Donghak (Eastern Learning) movement in 1860 as a syncretic Korean response to Catholicism — combining Confucian ethics, Buddhist meditation, Daoist cosmology, and shamanism — and was executed in 1864 for sedition, but the movement went underground and grew.

The Heungseon Daewongun ruled as regent for his son King Gojong from 1864 to 1873, gutted the seowon system, rebuilt Gyeongbokgung Palace, and enforced strict isolation — successfully repelling the General Sherman in 1866, the French in 1866, and the Americans in 1871.

In February 1876, Japan forced the Treaty of Ganghwa on Korea under the threat of gunboats — exactly mimicking Commodore Perry's 1854 opening of Japan, just one generation later — opening three Korean ports to Japanese trade and detaching Korea diplomatically from Qing China.

The Imo Incident of 1882 was a mutiny by old-style Joseon soldiers unpaid for over a year while Japanese-trained troops got new uniforms; the Gapsin Coup of 1884 was a three-day pro-Japanese seizure of the palace crushed by Qing troops under a young Yuan Shikai.

The Donghak Peasant Revolution of January 1894 became the largest peasant uprising in Joseon history, controlled most of Jeolla Province, and triggered both Qing and Japanese troop landings — directly causing the First Sino-Japanese War that ended Chinese suzerainty over Korea after five centuries.

On October 8, 1895, Japanese Minister Miura Goro orchestrated the murder of Queen Min by sword-armed assassins inside Gyeongbokgung Palace before dawn — and faced no meaningful punishment — proving that Joseon could no longer protect even its own monarchs.

King Gojong proclaimed himself Emperor of the Daehan Jeguk (Korean Empire) in 1897 after a year of refuge in the Russian legation, but the new empire was never really independent — Russian, Japanese, and American interests competed for Korea until Japan's victory in the Russo-Japanese War of 1904-05 made Japanese dominance final.

An Jung-geun assassinated former Japanese Resident-General Ito Hirobumi at Harbin railway station on October 26, 1909, with three close-range shots from a Browning automatic — the most consequential single act of Korean armed resistance, and a redeeming moment in the otherwise humiliating final years of Joseon.

On August 22, 1910, Japan formally annexed Korea, ending the Joseon dynasty after 518 years — one of the longest sustained runs of any single political system in human history, outlasting the Roman Empire, the Ming dynasty, the Tokugawa shogunate, and the Romanovs.
subtitle: Donghak, gunboats at Ganghwa, the murdered queen, and the annexation that ended 518 years of Joseon
